Name: HardwareAwarePrecisionOptimization
Title: Hardware-Aware Dynamic Precision Optimization for Efficient LLM Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic precision allocation based on real-time activation geometry can significantly reduce inference latency while maintaining accuracy, by leveraging hardware-specific profiling and optimization techniques.
Related Work: Recent works such as FlashInfer, SpInfer, and Coruscant have explored hardware-level optimizations for efficient LLM inference, focusing on custom attention engines, sparsity, and tensor core utilization. However, they primarily use static or heuristic approaches to precision and sparsity. Our proposal introduces a hardware-aware, dynamic precision optimization framework that adapts to real-time activation geometry, providing a more flexible and efficient solution.
Abstract: We propose Hardware-Aware Dynamic Precision Optimization (HADO), a framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision for large language model (LLM) inference based on real-time activation geometry. HADO leverages hardware-specific profiling to identify the optimal precision for each token, ensuring that well-conditioned activations are processed with lower precision, while ill-conditioned activations are handled with higher precision. By integrating with existing GPU profiling tools and kernel-level optimizations, HADO reduces inference latency while maintaining accuracy. We evaluate HADO on a variety of LLMs and benchmarks, demonstrating significant speedups and energy savings compared to static and heuristic precision allocation methods.
Experiments: 1. **Activation Geometry Analysis**: Collect activation states from a diverse set of LLMs during inference and perform singular value decomposition (SVD) to identify the spectral condition number and decay rate of hidden states.

2. **Precision Allocation Policy**: Develop and implement a precision allocation policy that maps spectral diagnostics to precision levels (e.g., 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit) for each token. Use randomized SVD for real-time computation.

3. **Kernel-Level Optimization**: Optimize CUDA kernels for mixed-precision operations, focusing on coalesced memory access, reduced HBM round-trips, and efficient register allocation. Ensure that the optimized kernels can handle dynamic precision switching.

4. **End-to-End Evaluation**: Compare the performance of HADO against static and heuristic precision allocation methods on a range of LLMs and benchmarks. Measure p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token. Use Nsight and nvprof to collect detailed hardware metrics.
5. **A/B Latency Races**: Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets to validate the effectiveness of HADO. Report p-values and confidence intervals derived from measured traces.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Real-Time Computation Overhead**: The real-time computation of spectral diagnostics and precision allocation may introduce additional overhead, which could negate the benefits of precision optimization.
2. **Model-Specific Behavior**: Different LLMs may exhibit varying activation geometries, requiring model-specific tuning of the precision allocation policy.
3. **Hardware Variability**: Performance may vary across different GPU architectures and driver versions, necessitating thorough cross-platform evaluation.
4. **Reproducibility**: Ensuring consistent and reproducible results across multiple runs and hardware configurations may be challenging, requiring careful benchmarking and trace collection.
